sourceTitle: Creating and organizing your reading list using Notion: A step-by-step guide

questions.question: What types of content can you track with a Notion reading list?

options.text: Only books
options.explanation: The guide says Notion can keep track of content you want to read, listen to, or watch—not just books.

options.text: Only videos
options.explanation: Videos are one of the formats you can track, but they are not the only ones.

options.text: Any content you want to read, listen to, or watch
options.explanation: Correct – the reading list works for books, articles, podcasts, videos, and other media.

options.text: Only images
options.explanation: Images can be captured by the web clipper, but they are not the primary item tracked.

questions.question: Where can you find a pre‑built template for a reading list in Notion?

options.text: The Template Gallery
options.explanation: The article directs users to browse the Template Gallery for ready‑made reading‑list templates.

options.text: Notion Settings > Integrations
options.explanation: Settings > Integrations is for connecting external services, not for template browsing.

options.text: The YouTube channel
options.explanation: YouTube provides tutorials, but templates are housed in the Template Gallery.

options.text: The Help Center search bar
options.explanation: The Help Center offers articles, not downloadable templates.

questions.question: Which of the following properties is NOT mentioned as a typical field in a Notion reading‑list database?

options.text: Date
options.explanation: Date fields are listed in the guide for tracking when you plan to read something.

options.text: Select menu
options.explanation: Select menus are explicitly mentioned as a property type.

options.text: Text
options.explanation: Text fields are a basic property for titles or notes.

options.text: Audio file upload
options.explanation: The article does not list an audio‑file upload property; it focuses on dates, selects, text, and links.

questions.question: How does applying a filter to your reading list make it more useful?

options.text: It changes the color scheme of the database
options.explanation: Filters affect which entries are shown, not the visual theme.

options.text: It hides entries that don’t meet the chosen criteria, such as items you’ve already read
options.explanation: Correct – filters let you view only the items you want, e.g., unread entries.

options.text: It automatically adds new articles from the web
options.explanation: Automatic addition is done by the web clipper, not by filters.

options.text: It syncs the list with external reading‑app accounts
options.explanation: Filters are a purely internal database feature; syncing is not mentioned.

questions.question: When you use Notion’s web clipper to add a webpage, what information does it capture?

options.text: Only the page URL
options.explanation: The clipper does more than just copy the URL.

options.text: The URL and the page title
options.explanation: Title is included, but the guide highlights that text and images are also saved.

options.text: The URL, plus all the page’s text and images
options.explanation: Exactly as described: the clipper grabs the URL, the article’s text, and any images.

options.text: The URL and a video playback of the page
options.explanation: Video playback isn’t part of the clipper’s functionality.

questions.question: If you want to see only the items you plan to read next month, which Notion feature would you primarily use?

options.text: Create a new page for next month’s items
options.explanation: Creating a new page is unnecessary; you can filter the existing database.

options.text: Apply a filter on the Date property to show entries within the next month
options.explanation: Filters on date properties let you narrow the view to a specific time range.

options.text: Change the reading‑list template to a monthly view
options.explanation: Templates define layout, not dynamic date‑based views.

options.text: Use the web clipper to add a ‘next month’ tag
options.explanation: While you could tag items, the question asks for the primary feature to filter by date.

questions.question: Why is a Notion reading list described as a “dynamic table,” and how does that differ from a static list?

options.text: Because it automatically updates its content from the internet without user input
options.explanation: Dynamic here refers to the database’s ability to be modified, not automatic internet updates.

options.text: Because it supports properties, filtering, sorting, and can be edited on the fly, unlike a static list that is just fixed text
options.explanation: Correct – a dynamic table lets you add properties, apply filters, sort, and change entries at any time.

options.text: Because it is a spreadsheet that cannot be edited once created
options.explanation: A spreadsheet can be edited, but the term “dynamic” emphasizes flexible views and properties, not immutability.

options.text: Because it is exported as a PDF that updates automatically
options.explanation: PDF export is a one‑time snapshot; it does not make the table dynamic.
